comment_content: The content is mainly the basic python programming knowledge, if you have learnt coding and programming before, it would be easy to handle, but i think with assistance of AI, there should be not difficulty on understanding the code. AI can handle all the content in this course. Topics are variable, type, for loop, while loop, recursion, class, oop.
comment_teaching: The teaching is fair, if you like to attend the lecture, go for it, COMP department professors are quite good at teaching, but if you have learnt all the concept of basic python programming, it would be a little bit necessary, since everything is easy, you can just search online or ask AI, i think that would be more efficient for learning. Remember practice is the key to success. Don't just read the lecture note and think you know everything, it doesn't work. At least type something by yourself.
comment_workload: The workload is fair, about 1 PA a week, the PA is easy to finish, just about the topic that you learn that week, nothing special, just remember to do it and everything would get full marks on that usually.
comment_grading: The grading is fair, since most of the student taking this course is from school of engineering, if you have no coding experience, it would be one the of biggest disadvantage by comparing to other student. But i think the key is to be careful enough, the content is easy but the exam content can be tricky. Understanding what the question requires you to implement is important, you can treat the exam as english reading exam, lots of words. Be aware of your time management. You may not able to finish the exam if you struggle in one particular questions, especially recursion.

comment_content:

- Installing python, the python shell, print(), input(), variables, type casting, the random module, python files.

- Functions

- Data types
- Turtle shapes, click and drag events, key events, the turtle coordinate system
- The RGB colour system
- State diagrams
- Dictionaries
- Objects
- Recursion

comment_content: With basic Python programming as the core, the content is relatively monotonous. The difficulty of the exam content does not match the difficulty of teaching. To achieve high scores, one must thoroughly review the material, otherwise it will be difficult
comment_teaching:
course website (http://course.cse.ust.hk/comp1021)
David is an exceptionally kind and responsible professor. I once emailed him in the middle of the night to inquire about the exam content, and he responded in just 10 minutes
comment_workload: There are a few assignments that are mandatory to participate in, while the rest are optional. The content is quite simple, and the instructions are very clear. Some assignments can even be perfectly completed by simply following the instructions rigorously. Additionally, online guidance is provided, making the difficulty very low.It should be noted that even if not mandatory, the homework that needs to be submitted will also be one of the topics for the exam. Therefore, even if you don't complete the homework, you still need to review it thoroughly.
comment_grading: There are two types of point systems. The first type is where both the midterm and final exams contribute to the final grade. The second type only requires the final exam score to be part of the final grade. The remaining portion is from assignments. I didn't take the midterm exam because it conflicted with other exams I had. There is no make-up exam mechanism for the midterm exam in this course. So, if you really want to fully participate in the entire course, you can discuss the situation with other course instructors to see if it's possible to reschedule other exams.

comment_content:
Content can be heavy if you do not have an existing background in python which is the focus of this course but only due to the difficulty in understanding the language from scratch.

Otherwise the course is not very content heavy and can be followed on your own if you start early enough. If it is difficult to understand the concepts then focus on memorizing instead since there is not enough time to completely learn the language and it does not exactly account for those with no history even though it is labelled as an introductory course.
comment_teaching:
Gibson is very passionate and encourages you to understand the concepts instead of memorizing - which is a better method if you are able to since that will take no memorizing head space at all plus you have unlimited cheat sheets.

He may go a bit fast at times but his content averages 20 slides per 50 minute lecture so it is not much to catch up on or ask about after class. He is also very open to answering questions and seems to have a real passion to teach
comment_workload: Assignments may take a few hours to complete for those who lack core knowledge already but assignments are not given weekly and is very useful as a form of studying the understanding of the code.
comment_grading:
Grading is heavy on the final but as long as you do well in your assignments(90%>) it isn't too hard to get a B as long as you study a decent amount for the exams themselves.

Calculation is also varies in your favour depending on whether you do better in your final or midterm(automatically)

comment_content: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to Python programming, starting from basic syntax and control structures (if–else statements, loops, functions) and gradually moving on to data structures such as lists, tuples, and dictionaries. The use of Turtle and PyGame libraries helps students understand graphical programming, event handling, and basic UI concepts, which makes the learning process more engaging. More advanced topics, including recursion, file input/output, and basic object-oriented programming, are briefly introduced toward the end. While not all Python features are covered, the scope is appropriate for an introductory-level course.
comment_teaching: Lectures are conducted entirely in English, using relatively simple language, although the speaking pace can be fast at times. The quality of teaching materials is high, especially the lecture PowerPoint slides, which are clear, well-organized, and closely aligned with the examinable content. The course website is well maintained and updated weekly. Lab instructors explain concepts step by step and often demonstrate code directly, which is particularly helpful for students with little or no programming background.
comment_workload: The overall workload is moderate and manageable. Most learning comes from reviewing lecture slides and understanding the provided code examples rather than extensive memorization. Labs are held weekly and are mostly ungraded, but they are useful for practicing core concepts and preparing for assignments. As long as students keep up with the weekly materials and do not leave assignments until the last minute, the time commitment required for this course is reasonable.
comment_grading: Grading is based on a mid-term exam, a final exam, and two assignments. Exams are open-book but still require a solid understanding of program structure, syntax, and data structures, especially list operations. Assignments are generally graded fairly and generously if the required functionality is correctly implemented. Overall, the grading scheme is reasonable, and students who consistently follow the course materials and practice coding should be able to achieve good results.

comment_content:
Teaches basic content about Python. Nothing is too difficult to understand, or makes you scratch your head in defeat. I won't say much as other reviews have already given much details.

However, the content difficulty is quite far away from that of COMP 1023. I suggest taking COMP 1028, a Python bridging course, if you want to take harder COMP courses.
comment_teaching:
I'd say James is a good professor. Quite humorous(dry humour), I enjoyed his lessons. I feel comfortable to do my own thing even though the class regularly has <20 people. Feel free to ask him any questions after class.

I sit one of Gibson's lessons, he is also a good prof and I can tell why his classes are popular. However, I personally prefer James' teaching style more. Gibson's voice felt more aggressive(?), and he talks quite fast, so a lot of info is entering my brain, which I struggle slightly to catch up. James' pace is more suitable for me.
comment_workload: There are two assignments that count for the course grade. The workload is manageable, with one lab per week, though not every lab needs to be submitted. Sometimes, the labs are related to the assignment. I suggest finishing the work given every week, whether or not it needs to be submitted. Instead of doing it in the last few days, because it is MUCH longer than you think... (This is how I got 20/100 for Assignment 2, please learn from my mistake)
comment_grading:
The midterm is quite simple, but I suspect they want everyone to fail their finals because WHAT WAS THAT???

Midterm: 78/100, average: 55.9, sd: 24.7
Finals: 47/100, average: 43.7, sd: 20.5
I got a B for this course, but I think I should've gotten at least B+ if I hadn't butchered my Assignment 2 with a 20/100.....

On the other hand, getting 100/100 for all your labs and assignments is very easy. As long as you follow the instructions and check the marking scheme carefully, full mark should be expected.

comment_content: 内容都是一些基本python语法，大概只有L-system和recursion两课是比较烧脑的了，这也是考试里面经常考的题目。整体来说，内容挺简单易懂，gibson的教学也很不错，虽然我只去了前几节课，大部分时间都是看lecture notes自学。不需要点名，没有出席率要求，lab只是教如何完成作业，可以不去。两个作业（各占10%），三个lab（各占5%），每份都挺有难度需要花一整天时间去完成，检查如果你不使用ai去做的话，但我也不推荐使用ai去做，最多使用ai去检查。每个问题多思考，做完每一年的pastpaper就足够了拿到B了，这个学期的两次考试的都是手写，期中简单且长，final难又长，应该绝大多数的人都来不及做完，要在适当的时候跳着做。

comment_content:
The course provides a comprehensive introduction to Python, covering fundamental topics such as variables, data types, control structures, functions, and file handling, which are essential for any beginner.
I especially appreciated the inclusion of practical problems and real-life examples, which helped make abstract concepts clearer and more applicable.
The course covers not only basic syntax but also introduces useful libraries like math and random, preparing students for more advanced topics.
comment_teaching:
The instructor explains concepts clearly, using step-by-step demonstrations which make it easy to follow for students with no programming background.
The balance between theory and hands-on coding exercises helped reinforce the material and encouraged active learning.
The teaching pace was well-suited for beginners—neither too fast nor too slow—and the instructor was responsive to students’ questions.

The grading scheme is designed to be student-friendly, offering two options to maximize your final score:

    Midterm (24%) + Lab Projects (36%) + Final Exam (40%)

    Lab Projects (42%) + Final Exam (58%)

    Lab Projects (3 hand-in): These are consistently highlighted as the easiest way to secure marks. With detailed instructions and video guides, achieving a near-perfect or perfect score is very common (means often in the high 80s to 90s). Tip: Meticulously check your code against the marking scheme, as silly errors can cost significant points.

comment_grading:
Labs 14% x 3 = 42%
Final = 58%

The lab are easy to get full marks as there are videos by TA and you can do them step by step.

comment_content: Basic skill of python.

comment_grading:
Two grading methods.
Method 1: midterm 24%, lab projects 36%, final exam 40%
Method 2: midterm 0%, lab projects 36%, final exam 40%

It’s easy to get a good grade. You can refer to your notes or textbooks during the midterm and final exams, but you still need to understand the concepts of programming first. Also, pay attention to the time. If you don’t know the answer of a question, skip it and come back to it later.

comment_content:
introductory programming course using Python 3 and PyGame as the foundation, covering both programming basics and simple UI concepts.
Programming basics, Python functions:
• Conditionals (if/elif/else), loops (while, for…in)
• Turtle library basics and drawing with loops
• PyGame MIDI and time control
• Data structures: list, tuple, dictionary
• List and string slicing, common methods (append, insert, remove, etc.)
• Functions, scope, recursion, stack
• File read/write, type()
• Dictionary operations
comment_teaching:
• Dedicated course website (http://course.cse.ust.hk/comp1021).
• Materials released one week in advance (color + B/W slides, code examples).
• Lab instructors carefully explain each step and often provide working code for reference.
comment_workload:
• Reading lecture slides and trying code examples is sufficient for ~90% in the mid-term and ~80% in the final.
• Preparing slides before class (hard/soft copy) is helpful, especially for beginners.
• Review after lectures should focus on syntax and structure.
comment_grading:
1. Mid-term Exam (20%) – open book, 1 hr, Week 7 (~40–45% of course).
• If skipped or if counting it lowers your grade, weight becomes 0%.
2. Final Exam (40%) – open book, 2.5 hr, covers the whole course.
• If no mid-term taken, or mid-term lowers your grade, weight becomes 60%.
3. Assignment 1: Music System (20%) – command-line program generating MIDI from text files.
• Lab 3 & 4 completion = 30% of A1; extra features = 70%.
4. Assignment 2: Game Project (20%) – Turtle-based “Space Invaders” game with event handling.
• Lab 7 & 8 completion = 34% of A2; extra features = 66%.
5. Assignment Bonus Point (2%) – awarded only if A1 + A2 < 40%. Earned by answering in-class questions (ping pong ball system).

comment_content:
It’s very easy if you have a programming background from high school.

They teach Python from the very beginning, basically just teaching you the basic syntax, like if and for loops. There will be more difficult coding in the lab projects, but the exam will be easy.
comment_teaching:
They use PowerPoint for all of the teaching in lectures, sometimes with a Python IDLE for demo. Not much special.

The lab will be more fun; they have video and written tutorials and guidelines to help you out.
comment_workload:
There isn’t much work to do. Only a few lab projects (6 to be exact).

The lab projects are writing tools and mini games. I’d say it’s quite fun to do them. They provide most of the code for you, so don’t worry; you just have to follow the video or written tutorial given by them to finish your project. It will only take 15-45 minutes to complete a project, depending on how well you know coding.
comment_grading:
Passing is easy, but if you want an A, it’s gonna take some effort.

The exam is online but not open book. Most of the questions are easy; a normal high school ICT student will know how to do them without attending the lecture. (Don’t suggest skipping lecture, though.)

If you want a higher grade, you’ll need to do more exercises, and there are a lot of exercises they provide on the course website.

comment_content: Really basic coding stuff written in Python.  I think the content is very appropriate in length and difficulty for a beginner's course on coding.  Even without any coding background anyone can easily pick up on the course's content.  For anyone with any sort of knowledge on programming the course will be a walk in the park .
comment_teaching: Didn't attend lectures much after the first few weeks.  But the lectures i attended are interesting to listen to and the professors are willing to answer questions.  The powerpoints are easy to understand so revision is easy.  The lab tasks came with detailed descriptions on how to tackle the tasks.  So even without attending to lectures you won't be too lost.  Also the course website provides us with many years of past paper during midterm and finals.

comment_content: This is a introductory computer course about Python programming. As it is for beginner, its content is not deep and complex.  However, if you are not good at or interested in programming, you will find it's complicated or boring. For me, it arouses my interest in game programming, as there is a assignment about creating a treasure game.
comment_teaching: Every lesson has 50 mins only and it will be a new chapter, so the pitch is a little bit fast, especially to those who have not ever learn programming before. The teaching way is like a talk, explaining the ppt and sometimes demonstrates through the Python. However, it is somewhat difficult to understand. Besides lecture, there are tutorial video and zoom class. These tutorial classes are for homework guidance so they are recommended. Also, watch recordings after each lecture and do exercises released from each lecture. Practice will help for your understanding.
comment_workload:
There are 5 programming assignments. Remember to hand in those assignments on time.  One midterm and one final exam. Also, there are two lecture classes each week, and each lesson is 50 mins only. There are also tutorial class. However, all the lecture and tutorial classes didn't take attendance and there are recordings. Therefore, the workload is super low.
But to get a better performance in midterm and final exam, do past exam paper as much as you can. The format is similar within  years. You will find those past paper from CANVAS, the instructor will release for you.
comment_grading: The Homework assignments account for a great percentage to the overall grading. Most of students will get full mark so the overall grading will not be bad. But remember to hand in the HW on time, as late for 1 second will be graded as 0.  Other percentage goes to Midterm and Final Exam. There are two grading scheme : count both midterm and final exam in or only count final exam . Therefore, if you perform not well in midterm, you will another chance in final. Also, the median for midterm and final is 50% to 60% only. If you meets median and get full mark in assignments, you will get at least B or B+.

comment_content:
Fundamental Python Concepts:
Covers essentials such as if/else statements, for loops, lists, dictionaries, basic classes, methods/functions, and recursion.

Turtle Graphics:
Introduces the Turtle graphics library, which allows you to create drawings by controlling a "turtle" (think of it as a pen or brush) using code.

Difficulty:
The material should be very straightforward if you have any prior programming experience. However, take care with data types—Python is dynamically typed (and you won’t be using or tested on type hints in exams), so watch out for type-related pitfalls.
comment_teaching: The teacher has a great sense of humor, and the class is generally quite enjoyable. However, it can sometimes take extra effort to fully grasp what he's teaching.
comment_workload:
Personally, I completed all the self-exercises and practiced with all the past papers for both the midterm and final exams. It can be time-consuming, but it’s extremely helpful—I highly recommend doing all of them, as the exam questions are often quite similar.

The midterm is relatively easy, so aim to get a perfect score. However, the final exam is much more challenging; make sure you prepare thoroughly and pay close attention!
comment_grading:
Overall, I’m quite pleased with my performance. Although I lost around 10% on the assignments, I still managed to finish strong.

Final Exam Statistics:

Average: ~62.3%
Standard Deviation: ~17.8%
Highest Score: 98.5%
Lowest Score: ~12.4%
There are two grading systems: one includes the midterm, and one doesn’t. Using the first scheme, my scores break down as follows:

Assignments (36% of total): 88.5%
Midterm (24% of total): 95%
Final Exam (40% of total): 82%
Final Score: 87.1%
Letter Grade: A
